chunks.page_summary: This support article from Rheonics details how to directly connect Rheonics sensors to a computer using a straight-through Ethernet cable, addressing product involvement and setup requirements.
chunks.extracted_text:
Phone: +1 713 234 0863

Can I connect to the Rheonics Sensor using a straight-through ethernet cable directly from the computer?

Modified on: Fri, 30 Jan, 2026 at 3:22 PM

Which Products are Involved?
SRV (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-viscometer-srv/), SRD (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-density-meter-srd/), DVM (https://rheonics.com/products/hpht-pvt-density-meter-viscometer-dvm/), SME-DRM (https://rheonics.com/electronics-and-communication/).
For SME-DRM (DIN rail mount) transmitter. Visit Rheonics product page: https://rheonics.com/products/ (https://rheonics.com/products/)

1. Can I connect using an ethernet cable directly from my computer or laptop?
Rheonics ethernet-enabled sensor can connect with straight-through cables to ethernet ports that support Auto MDI-X after setting the correct IP address and Subnet mask. To correctly set the IP address and subnet mask on the SME (sensor electronics), check this support article https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000397449-connecting-your-sme-using-ethernet

2. What to do if I want to connect to the sensor connected over LAN?
This article is not for you, refer to the article for LAN connections (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/folders/81000219061) or check our RCP manual Rheonics Control Panel Section 4.1.2. (https://rheonics.com/wp-content/uploads/dlm_uploads/2019/09/Rheonics_RCP_Manual.pdf)

3. How do I connect to sensor SME electronics using an ethernet cable plugged directly into my computer?
The connection between the SME and the PC directly with a single Ethernet cable is only possible if the PC ethernet device (NIC) supports Auto-MDI-X. In figure 1 it is shown how both the SME and a Laptop are connected and receive data through the RCP software.
key_facts: Rheonics ethernet-enabled sensors can be connected directly to a computer or laptop using straight-through Ethernet cables, provided the computer's ethernet port supports Auto MDI-X.
Correct IP address and Subnet mask must be set on the SME (sensor electronics) for a direct connection.
For connecting to a sensor over a LAN, users are advised to refer to a dedicated article for LAN connections or the Rheonics Control Panel (RCP) manual.
The connection between the SME and a PC/laptop allows data reception through the RCP software.
Involved products include SRV (inline viscometer), SRD (inline density meter), DVM (HPHT-PVT density meter), and SME-DRM (DIN rail mount) transmitter.
warnings_notes: This article is not for connections over LAN; refer to the article for LAN connections or the RCP manual.

key_facts: The document provides instructions on how to access and modify network settings within a Windows environment.
The first step involves viewing 'Network Connection Details' which includes IPv4 and IPv6 configuration.
The second step instructs the user to right-click a 'Local Area connection' (specifically 'Ethernet') and select 'Properties' from the context menu.
The network adapter in the example is identified as an ASIX AX88772B USB2.0 to Fast Ethernet.
The example IPv4 address is 192.168.62.17 with a subnet mask of 255.255.255.0 and a default gateway and DNS server also set to 192.168.62.17.
The final instruction is to double click 'Internet Protocol Version 4 (TCP/IPv4)' to proceed with configuration.

Ethernet MAC
MAC Address is the physical address that identifies a device on the same local network. This value cannot be modified.
DHCP
It is recommended to set this value as false if we are looking to integrate the sensor alongside other controllers. If the value is left to a true value, to identify the assigned IP address it is necessary the support from the IT department of your company.
IP
IP address is a unique address that identifies a device on the internet or a local network. This address should not be duplicated on the same network to avoid conflict with the devices and is normally set by the integrator or IT department.
key_facts: The Rheonics Control Panel (RCP) software version is 5.3.1.6.

Leaving DHCP as 'true' may require IT department support to identify the assigned IP address.
An IP address is a unique identifier for a device on a network and should not be duplicated to prevent conflicts.
The Subnet Mask must be identical for both the PC and the SME device.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides definitions and configuration instructions for Ethernet network parameters on an SME device using RCP software.
chunks.extracted_text:
Subnet
Subnet mask is a number that distinguishes the network address and the host address within an IP address. A subnet is a smaller network within a network that requires a subnet mask. This value depends on the IP class to be used normally, the most common one is Class C with 255.255.255.0

Gateway IP
A gateway is a network node that serves as an access point to another network. This value is commonly set to 0.0.0.0 if no access to other networks is required. Commonly used when we are going to use routers in our network.

DNS
DNS stands for the Domain Name System, connecting domain names to IP addresses. Value can be set to 0.0.0.0 if not used.

Terminal Port
The default port number used is 10001 for Telnet service.

Ethernet
Ethernet MAC: 70:B3:D5:D2:03:92
DHCP: False
IP: 192.168.62.20
Subnet: 255.255.255.0
Gateway IP: 192.168.62.20
DNS: 192.168.62.20
Terminal Port: 10001

Figure 11. Ethernet Parameters configured on the SME using RCP software

5.3. Click on the "Write config" button.
key_facts: A subnet mask distinguishes the network address and the host address within an IP address.
A subnet is a smaller network within a network that requires a subnet mask.
The subnet mask value depends on the IP class, with Class C commonly using 255.255.255.0.
A gateway is a network node that provides an access point to another network.
Gateway IP is commonly set to 0.0.0.0 if no access to other networks is required and is used when routers are present.
DNS stands for the Domain Name System, which connects domain names to IP addresses.
The DNS value can be set to 0.0.0.0 if not used.
The default port number for Telnet service is 10001.
Ethernet Parameters for the SME device are configured using RCP software.
To apply configuration changes, the "Write config" button must be clicked.

6. How do I connect the PC and SME?

6.1. Next is to connect the same Ethernet cable to the PC and the SME. Be aware that most PC only has one ethernet port, so LAN connectivity won't be possible over ethernet anymore (often wireless connections are available in PC which can be used during this time for internet connectivity if needed - RCP does not need internet connectivity for normal functioning). Find the Ethernet port on your computer and connect the ethernet cable.
key_facts: The Rheonics Control Panel version is 5.3.1.6.
The software provides settings for Modbus RTU, LCD Display (SME-TRD Only), Ethernet, Location ID, and Bluetooth LTE 4.0.
Operation history includes RTC time, last power reset, supply voltage, SME temperature, and maximum sensor/SME temperature exposure.
The Rheonics Control Panel (RCP) does not require internet connectivity for normal functioning.
To connect a PC and SME, an Ethernet cable is used to connect the Ethernet port on the PC to the SME.
warnings_notes: Be aware that most PC only has one ethernet port, so LAN connectivity won't be possible over ethernet anymore (often wireless connections are available in PC which can be used during this time for internet connectivity if needed - RCP does not need internet connectivity for normal functioning).

7. How do I use RCP software to connect to the Rheonics sensor over Ethernet?

7.1. Once the Ethernet cable is connected on each end, it would be necessary to enable Ethernet communication. Set the IP address in RCP that you configured for the SME, go to the setting tab and select Ethernet in the radio button, fill in the same IP address you used in the “Communication” tab, and click the “Apply” button.
key_facts: The document illustrates how to connect an Ethernet cable to the Rheonics SME device.
To enable Ethernet communication, the Ethernet cable must be connected on both ends.
Users must set the IP address in the RCP software to match the IP address configured for the SME.
The process involves navigating to the 'setting tab' in RCP, selecting 'Ethernet' via a radio button, filling in the IP address in the 'Communication' tab, and clicking 'Apply'.
The device shown is labeled 'SME-DRM' and is 'swiss made'.
The SME-DRM device supports 24 V DC power input, Modbus (RS485) communication, 4-20 mA analog output, USB, display, and SD-card connectivity, and has a multi-pin sensor connection.

The software provides tabs for Measurement, Events, Settings, Service, Communication, Logger, and Help.

After completing settings, users should navigate to the 'measurement tab' to verify data reception.

Figure 16. Measurements were received through Ethernet Cable.

8. What are MDI/MDIX and Auto MDI-X?

The MDI/MDIX are Ethernet interfaces (physical and electrical/optical) in a computer network that carry transmissions. Twisted pair cables should be connected so that the transmission pair on one end is connected to the receiving pair on the other. [1]

The MDI (Medium Dependent Interface) is an Ethernet port connection that connects to the NIC (Network Interface Card) or Integrated NIC port on a PC. Input and output signals on a NIC must be redirected to receiving signals on a hub or network switch. Thus, the latter devices use MDIX to switch transmission and receiving signals – X here stands for crossover, indicating that inputs and outputs are reversed.

[Figure 17: MDI PC to MDI Device setup diagram]
Figure 17. MDI PC to MDI Device setup.

In routers, hubs, and network switches, MDIX (Medium Dependent Interface Crossover). A straight-through cable connects pins 1 and 2 (transmitting) of an MDI device to pins 1 and 2 (receiving) of an MDIX device, and vice versa for pins 3 and 6 (receiving) of an MDI device to pins 3 and 6 (transmitting) of an MDIX device. MDIX eliminates the need for crossover twisted pair cabling in this case.
key_facts: Measurements for the Rheonics Control Panel can be received through an Ethernet Cable.
MDI/MDIX refers to Ethernet interfaces (physical and electrical/optical) that carry transmissions in a computer network.
Twisted pair cables require a connection where the transmission pair on one end connects to the receiving pair on the other.
MDI (Medium Dependent Interface) is an Ethernet port used for connecting to a NIC (Network Interface Card) or integrated NIC on a PC.
Input and output signals on a NIC must be redirected to receiving signals when connected to a hub or network switch.
MDIX is utilized by hubs or network switches to switch transmission and receiving signals, with 'X' signifying crossover and reversed inputs/outputs.
MDIX (Medium Dependent Interface Crossover) in network devices like routers and hubs eliminates the need for crossover twisted pair cabling.
A straight-through cable connects transmitting pins 1 and 2 of an MDI device to receiving pins 1 and 2 of an MDIX device.
A straight-through cable also connects receiving pins 3 and 6 of an MDI device to transmitting pins 3 and 6 of an MDIX device.

chunks.page_summary: This page explains MDI/MDIX interfaces, cable types for Ethernet connections, and the Auto MDI-X feature.
chunks.extracted_text:
Figure 18. MDI PC to Hub MDI-x.

The MDI interface is used by end stations such as PCs and workstations, while the MDIX interface is used by hubs and network switches. Often, routers have multiple MDIX ports and a single MDI port and to connect to other hubs or switches without a crossover Ethernet cable, some network hubs and switches have an MDI port. The MDI and MDIX ports are generally connected via straight-through twisted pair cables. Crossover twisted pair cables are used for MDI-to-MDI and MDIX-to-MDIX connections.

Auto MDI-X is the solution to getting the wrong cable for any configuration, it will automatically detect cable type and configures the connection accordingly avoiding the issue of getting the wrong cable. Both types of cables can be used as long as the feature is enabled at either end of the link. [2]

| Setting         | MDI/MDIX Device Type (PC or other MDI device) | MDI/MDIX Device Type (Switch, hub, or other MDIX device) |
|-----------------|---------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|
| MDI             | Crossover cable                             | Straight-through cable                                   |
| MDIX            | Straight-through cable                      | Crossover cable                                          |
| Auto-MDI/MDIX   | Either crossover or straight-through cable  | Either crossover or straight-through cable               |

9. How do I check my computer meets the requirements for the Ethernet cable direct connection?

*   Ethernet card datasheet

Datasheet for the NIC should indicate if it is compatible with auto-MDI-x
key_facts: The MDI interface is used by end stations (e.g., PCs, workstations).
The MDIX interface is used by hubs and network switches.
Routers often feature multiple MDIX ports and a single MDI port.
MDI and MDIX ports are typically connected with straight-through twisted pair cables.
Crossover twisted pair cables are used for MDI-to-MDI and MDIX-to-MDIX connections.
Auto MDI-X automatically detects and configures the cable type, preventing incorrect cable usage.
With Auto MDI-X enabled, both crossover and straight-through cables can be used.
To check computer compatibility for direct Ethernet connection, consult the Ethernet card datasheet.
The Network Interface Card (NIC) datasheet should specify auto-MDI-X compatibility.
diagram_labels: Figure 18. MDI PC to Hub MDI-x.

10. FAQ/Troubleshooting
key_facts: The Network Interface Card (NIC) has a 2-year warranty and supports PCI Express.
The NIC uses the Realtek - RTL8111H chipset and is compliant with several IEEE 802.3 standards.
It provides one RJ45 Gigabit Ethernet port with Auto MDIX and Full Duplex support.
The NIC is compatible with 10/100/1000 Mbps networks and supports a maximum data transfer rate of 1000 Mbps.
Jumbo Frames up to 9K max are supported.
The NIC is compatible with Windows operating systems from 2000 to 10.
The 1000BASE-T standard was promoted by Daniel Joseph Dove.
Some Fast Ethernet devices might incorporate Auto MDI-X functionality.
For proper operation, network interfaces are required to be configured for auto-negotiation of speed and duplex.
Configuration for auto-negotiation can be verified and set within the Ethernet card properties under the Advanced tab.
diagram_labels: Realtek PCIe GbE Family Controller Properties
warnings_notes: It requires that the interfaces be configured to auto-negotiate speed and duplex. This can be identified if we go to the Ethernet card properties>Advanced tab.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides a troubleshooting guide for common Ethernet connectivity issues, specifically related to pinging devices, MDI-X support, and IP configuration, including references and a disclaimer.
chunks.extracted_text:
Problem
1. Ethernet cable is connected between my PC and the SME, but cannot ping the address
2. My PC support auto MDI-X, but still not getting data
3. How do I check if my PC supports auto MDI-X?
4. Which ethernet cable should I use crossover cable to straight-through cable?
5. Is there a software that I can use to verify if my ethernet card supports auto MDI-x

Solution
1. Verify the IP address for each device is different and Subnet Mask is the same
2. Your PC might not support auto MDI-X
3. Try changing the crossover cable to a straight-through cable or vice-versa.
4. Verify that you can ping the address with the console command ping AAA.BBB.CCC.DDD>IP address

1. Verify that the SME has a static IP address and that DHCP is set as false.
2. Verify that you can ping the address with the console command ping AAA.BBB.CCC.DDD>IP address

1. Verify the datasheet from your Ethernet card, it should mention if Auto MDI-x is supported or not.

4. Any of the two configurations is compatible with Auto MDI-x

5. Some manufacturers provide software that can provide the characteristics of the Ethernet card. Users can verify this by looking at the datasheet or checking if the system is Gigabit Ethernet.

References
1. https://community.fs.com/blog/mdi-vs-mdix-and-auto-mdim
2. http://www.hp.com/hpinfo/abouthp/iplicensing/automdix.html [2]
3. https://assets.alliedelec.com/v1590663186/Datasheets/fef893262b7bbaf1d59632d3c3c77bec.pdf [3]
4. https://www.ieee802.org/3/ab/public/feb98/ddmdix1.pdf [4]
5. https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000397449-connecting-your-smet-using-ethernet
6. https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000300292-introducing-ethernet-connections-on-smet

key_facts: To troubleshoot inability to ping a device (SME) over Ethernet, verify distinct IP addresses and matching subnet masks, check for Auto MDI-X support, try different cable types (crossover/straight-through), and use the `ping` command.
If a PC supports Auto MDI-X but still isn't receiving data, ensure the SME has a static IP address with DHCP disabled and verify ping connectivity.
Auto MDI-X support for an Ethernet card can be confirmed by reviewing its datasheet.
Either crossover or straight-through Ethernet cables are compatible when using Auto MDI-X.
Software from manufacturers or checking the datasheet/Gigabit Ethernet status can help verify an Ethernet card's characteristics, including Auto MDI-X support.
